coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of color-tunable carbon dots-based organic long persistent luminescence materials
coredata.dc:creator: Di, Yujie
Liu, Weifeng
Shi, Shuhao
Wu, Tao
Wang, Meiling
Liu, Xuguang
coredata.dc:description: The use of carbon dots (CDs) to construct binary donor–acceptor exciplex systems has been shown to be an effective method for obtaining organic long persistent luminescence (OLPL). However, such a system currently necessitates the prior synthesis of CDs before integration with the organic matrix, thus complicating the reproducibility and large-scale production of the materials. Furthermore, attaining color-tunable long persistent luminescence (LPL) is also a major challenge for the system. Here, we report a one-step synthesis strategy for color-tunable CDs-based OLPL material (named CDs@CA). CDs donors and CA acceptors in the material can be generated in a single step during the reaction. Under ambient conditions and even in aqueous media, the LPL color of CDs@CA can be changed by the excitation wavelength, enabling color tuning from blue to green emission. This is the first time for the color tuning to be achieved in CDs-based OLPL materials. Meanwhile, the strategy is applicable to a wide range of alternative carbon sources, which makes it possible to rapidly prepare a wide range of CDs-based OLPL materials with varying LPL performance by selecting different carbon sources. This work is greatly beneficial to the development and application of CDs-based OLPL systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Long persistent luminescence
Carbon dots
Exciplex
Charge separation
Multicolor emissions

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs@CA-a, CDs@CA-b, CDs@CA-c, and CDs@CA-d

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Central Leading Science and Technology Development Foundation of Shanxi Province
Key R&D Program of Shanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51972221 ), Central Leading Science and Technology Development Foundation of Shanxi Province (YDZJSX2022A009), Key R&D Program of Shanxi Province (International Cooperation, 201903D421077).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Exciplex emission from the binary donor–acceptor exciplex systems. During photoexcitation, electrons (black sphere) on the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the acceptor are excited to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) (i); electrons from the HOMO of the donor continuously fill the HOMO of the acceptor, forming charge-transfer (CT) states (ii); the donor forms a radical cation owing to the electron loss and the acceptor forms a radical anion owing to electron gain. Then, the acceptor radical anions diffuse to form charge-separated (CS) states (iii); after a period of diffusion, the radical anions and radical cations gradually recombine (iv) to produce exciplex emission (v). b) A possible LPL mechanism. Rapid charge recombination occurs in the early phase of LPL (i); the luminescence process is able to continue owing to electrons recombining with holes by jumping through several neighboring molecules (ii); electrons jump through more neighboring molecules, resulting in a longer luminescence duration (iii).

a) schematic illustration of CDs@CA preparation process; b–d) TEM images of CDs@CA-1, the insert image in (d) is HRTEM image of the area of red circle. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

a) photographs of cds@ca-1 powder under ambient sunlight, excited with uv light, and at different delay times after switching off the excitation light (excitation power, 10 W; excitation time, 1 min; temperature, 298.15 K); b) Log-log plots of the emission decay profiles of CDs@CA-1 powder at 275 nm excitation wavelength. Insert: Corresponding afterglow emission photograph; c) Log-log plots of the emission decay profiles of CDs@CA-1 powder at 365 nm excitation wavelength. Insert: Corresponding afterglow emission photograph; d) FL emission spectra and afterglow emission spectra at 275 nm and 365 nm excitation wavelengths for CDs@CA-1 powder; e) Afterglow excitation spectra of CDs@CA-1 monitored at different emission wavelengths; f) Excitation time-dependent LPL logarithmic decay profiles; g) Ratio of IPA to urea-dependent LPL logarithmic decay profiles.

a) XRD patterns of CDs@CA-1 and CDs@CA-2; b) FT-IR spectra of CDs@CA-1 and CDs@CA-2; c) XPS spectra of CDs@CA-1 and CDs@CA-2; d–f) high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (d), N 1 s (e), and O 1 s (f) in CDs@CA-1 and CDs@CA-2; g) Log-log plots of the emission decay profiles of CDs@CA-2 and CDs#CA-2 powder at 275 nm excitation wavelength; h) XRD patterns of CDs@CA-2 and CDs#CA-2; i) Log-log plots of the emission decay profiles of CDs@CA-a, CDs@CA-b, CDs@CA-c, and CDs@CA-d powders at optimal excitation wavelength.

a) Photographs of CDs@CA-1 fully immersed in water in daylight and after the UV lamp was turned off (excitation wavelength, 275 nm (top) and 365 nm (bottom); excitation power, 10 W; excitation time, 1 min; temperature, 298.15 K); b) Photographs of the continuous display of information presented by CDs@CA-1; c) Real-time images of a fingerprint made of CDs@CA-1 powders.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of color-tunable carbon dots-based organic long persistent luminescence materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of New Carbon Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of New Carbon Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of carbon dots (CDs) to construct binary donor–acceptor exciplex systems has been shown to be an effective method for obtaining organic long persistent luminescence (OLPL). However, such a system currently necessitates the prior synthesis of CDs before integration with the organic matrix, thus complicating the reproducibility and large-scale production of the materials. Furthermore, attaining color-tunable long persistent luminescence (LPL) is also a major challenge for the system. Here, we report a one-step synthesis strategy for color-tunable CDs-based OLPL material (named CDs@CA). CDs donors and CA acceptors in the material can be generated in a single step during the reaction. Under ambient conditions and even in aqueous media, the LPL color of CDs@CA can be changed by the excitation wavelength, enabling color tuning from blue to green emission. This is the first time for the color tuning to be achieved in CDs-based OLPL materials. Meanwhile, the strategy is applicable to a wide range of alternative carbon sources, which makes it possible to rapidly prepare a wide range of CDs-based OLPL materials with varying LPL performance by selecting different carbon sources. This work is greatly beneficial to the development and application of CDs-based OLPL systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Long persistent luminescence (LPL) materials are materials that can emit afterglow for a long time after the excitation source is turned off [1–3]. Owing to their excellent optical properties, they are widely used in various fields such as glow-in-the-dark coatings [4], bio-imaging [5,6], solar cells [7,8], and optical data storage [9]. They store excitation energy in the excited states and release this energy as light slowly [10]. In 1996, Matsuzawa et al. discovered the green inorganic LPL material SrAl2O4:Eu2+, Dy3+ [11]. Since then, research of inorganic LPL materials has rapidly advanced. The luminous duration of these materials in the darkness has evolved from seconds or minutes to hours or even days, and the optical properties of LPL such as intensity, efficiency, and the range of emitting wavelengths have been extensively investigated [12–20]. Nevertheless, the requirement of rare elements (e.g., europium and dysprosium) and harsh synthesis conditions in the preparation process undoubtedly raises the cost of using inorganic LPL materials [21,22].

To address this problem, researchers have developed organic long persistent luminescence (OLPL) materials. These materials do not require rare elements, are color-tunable, and are easier to process compared with conventional materials [1,23–28]. In 2017, Adachi et al. reported the first OLPL system through two simple organic molecules, 2,8-bis(diphenylphosphoryl)dibenzo[b,d] thiophene (PPT) as an electron acceptor and N,N,N′,N′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a strong electron donor, the mixture of which produces very robust sustained LPL emission [10]. This method of obtaining charge separated (CS) states by binary donor–acceptor exciplex systems is by far the most successful for generating OLPL systems. In such systems, light induces the generation of donor radical cations and acceptor radical anions, thereby causing the formation of CS states [29]. The recombination of radical cations with radical anions produces exciplex emission (Fig. 1 a), and the progressive recombination of a large number of radical cations with radical anions leads to LPL production (Fig. 1b). Such binary donor–acceptor exciplex systems are not limited to purely organic systems, and binary donor–acceptor exciplex systems in which non-organic materials act as donors or acceptors are currently being developed [30,31].

Carbon dots (CDs) are quasi-spherical carbon nanomaterials that are less than 10 nm in size [32,33]. They have been of significant research interest since their nomenclature in 2004 through arc-discharge synthesis as a result of their excellent photophysical properties [34]. CDs can be prepared easily and cost-effectively, and they are also environmentally friendly [35–42]. More impressively, CDs afterglow materials with various unique optical properties have also been developed in recent years [43–50]. In 2022, Lin et al. developed the first OLPL system based on CDs by embedding m-CDs (prepared from m-phenylenediamine) in cyanuric acid (CA), which is free of rare elements, emits hourly-level blue afterglow, and is free from the limitations of OLPL systems that require preparation in a nitrogen atmosphere [31]. The latest breakthrough in CDs-based OLPL systems indicates the possibility of using CDs as electron donors to construct LPL systems [31,51]. However, current CDs-based OLPL materials require the prior preparation of CDs materials and then embedment of them in organic electron acceptor materials, which undoubtedly complicates the reproducibility and large-scale production of CDs-based OLPL materials. If the generation of CDs and their assembly with a suitable organic substrate can be achieved directly in a single step, the whole production process would be greatly simplified. It should be noted that the current CDs-based OLPL materials are only limited to blue LPL, which is not conducive to the application of CDs-based OLPL materials, and the other colors of LPL have yet to be developed.

Here, we present a one-step strategy for synthesizing color-tunable CDs-based OLPL material (named CDs@CA), in which CDs donors and CA acceptors are directly generated during a one-step melting reaction. The material exhibits strong LPL properties, with CDs@CA producing a blue afterglow visible to the naked eye for up to 60 min when irradiated at 275 nm with a conventional handheld UV lamp. Interestingly, when the emission wavelength of the UV lamp was switched to 365 nm, five minutes of green afterglow emission was observed. Further studies have demonstrated that CA is generated in situ from urea during the heating process and functions as an electron acceptor in the binary donor–acceptor exciplex system, and its proportion in the system is crucial to LPL. The tunability of LPL color is attributed to the presence of multiple emission centers in CDs@CA, where certain emission centers dominate the afterglow emission as the excitation wavelength is varied, resulting in different colors of LPL emission. Notably, the synthesis strategy is universally applicable, and LPL materials with different optical properties can be prepared depending on the choice of carbon source.

Color-tunable OLPL materials based on CDs were prepared by a simple one-step melting method. In brief, a small quantity of isophthalic acid (IPA) was added to urea and pyrolyzed for 5 h at 195 °C, and then blue-green dual-emitting OLPL materials (i.e., CDs@CA) were obtained (Fig. 2 a). CDs and CA are generated simultaneously during the reaction. Thus, CDs are confined in situ in the CA matrix. As shown in Figures S1 and S2 in the Supplementary Information, the material possesses better optical properties when the ratio of IPA to urea is 0.3 wt% (IPA:urea by weight), so we used CDs@CA-1 (0.3 wt%) as the object of this study. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images show that CDs were synthesized and embedded in a rigid matrix (Fig. 2b–d). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in insets of Fig. 2d shows well-resolved crystal lattices of the carbon cores, with the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [52,53]. When only urea was used, the synthesized material, called UR@CA-0, did not exhibit any CDs in its TEM images (Figure S3, Supporting Information).

The optical properties of CDs@CA-1 were analyzed. As shown in Figure S4 (Supporting Information), CDs@CA-1 has a strong absorption at 215 nm, which are attributed to the π-π* leaps in the sp2-π conjugation domain [54]. Both photoluminescence (PL) spectra and afterglow emission spectra indicate the optimal excitation wavelength of CDs@CA-1 at 275 nm and optimal emission at 438 nm (Figure S5, Supporting Information). The temperature-dependent afterglow emission spectra of CDs@CA-1 show a gradual reduction in afterglow intensity as temperature increases from 80 K to 360 K, indicating its phosphorescent nature (Figure S6, Supporting Information) [52]. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield and phosphorescence quantum yield of CDs@CA-1 are 11.2 % and 10.4 %, respectively. The phosphorescence lifetime at 438 nm was also found to be up to 1.61 s (Figure S7 and Table 1, Supporting Information). CDs@CA-1 shows blue fluorescence emission when irradiated with 275 or 365 nm UV lamps. Surprisingly, CDs@CA-1 shows a blue LPL for 60 min after the 275 nm UV light is turned off, and a green LPL for 5 min when the excitation light source is changed to 365 nm UV (Fig. 3 a). If the decay spectrum is measured, the afterglow of CDs@CA-1 can last for 100 min under 275 nm excitation wavelength and 8 min under 365 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 3b and 3c). This is completely different from the seconds-level of afterglow emission of UR@CA-0 prepared only with urea, indicating the construction of OLPL system by the introduction of CDs (Figures S8 and S9, Supporting Information). As far as we know, this is the first reported instance of color-tunable LPL for OLPL systems based on CDs.

Next, the multicolor emission of CDs@CA-1 were investigated. In Fig. 3d, switching the excitation wavelength from 275 nm to 365 nm results in a change in CDs@CA-1′s PL emission peak from 438 nm to 400 nm, the afterglow emission peak shifts from 438 nm to 478 nm, and the afterglow color changes from blue to green. The calculation of chromaticity coordinate (CIE) also confirms the change of the afterglow color (Figure S10, Supporting Information). CDs@CA-1 contains a large amount of CA (this will be demonstrated later). It is well known that CA can show green afterglow emission after UV excitation. However, we discarded the possibility that the green afterglow of CDs@CA-1 is derived from CA by analyzing the afterglow emission spectra of CA, as the afterglow emission peak positions of the two are dissimilar (Figure S11, Supporting Information). To clarify the source of excitation-dependent afterglow in CDs@CA-1, we monitored the afterglow excitation spectra of CDs@CA-1 at various emission wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 3e, they all have an excitation peak at 275 nm and a gradually stronger excitation peak at 290 to 390 nm when the emission wavelength is larger. The observation implies that CDs@CA-1 has multiple emission centers [55]. Depending on the excitation wavelength, certain centers become dominant and show different colors in the afterglow emission. Unfortunately, the origin of these emission centers is unknown because the CDs could not be isolated individually.

The LPL decay spectrum of CDs@CA-1 at 275 nm excitation wavelength is scrutinized, and the afterglow intensity of CDs@CA-1 decays exponentially within 0.3 min and then obeys the inverse power function of time t−1 (Fig. 3b). According to the interpretation of the Debye-Edward law (t−m, with m = 1), the t−1 decay profile is consistent with the presence of CS intermediate states produced by photo-irradiation [56]. Thus, the t−1 decay profile of CDs@CA-1 suggests the presence of CS intermediate states during UV irradiation, which may be responsible for the observed cause of the LPL.

To further confirm the presence of the CS state in CDs@CA-1, we examined in detail the persistent luminescence that is dependent on excitation time and IPA content. As shown in Fig. 3f, the LPL duration increases with excitation time. The emission duration is only 15 min after 5 s of UV excitation, while it reaches 100 min after 1 min of excitation. When the excitation time is longer (more than 3 min), the LPL intensity and duration remain almost unchanged. Typically, the duration of CS state-based LPL materials is related to the excitation time, following the charge storage mechanism [3,10,31]. The number of carriers in the material accumulates with excitation time, which is macroscopically manifested as an increase in LPL duration. And when the number of carriers accumulated in the material reaches the upper limit, the LPL intensity and duration no longer change. The excitation time-dependent LPL duration behavior is another evidence of carrier production and accumulation in materials under UV lamp irradiation. Then, the LPL duration associated with IPA contents was investigated. In Fig. 3g, a significant decrease is observed in LPL duration with increasing IPA content. This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that the proportion of CDs in the sample increases with the increase of IPA content, which leads to the weakening of the separation degree of CS state and the shortening of the time required for charge recombination [10,31], which is macroscopically manifested as the decrease of LPL duration, as can be proved in the next part. In addition, it can be speculated that the excitation power has an effect on the LPL duration. In Fig. 3a, CDs@CA-1 is the first to darken at the edge position of the sample with time after excitation by the handheld UV lamp. This is due to the uneven power distribution of the handheld UV lamp (the power at the center position is greater than that at the edge position). Finally, the existence of radical species after photoexcitation was confirmed by electron-spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy, which provides direct evidence for the presence of radicals in CDs@CA-1 (Figure S12, Supporting Information) [29,31].

Owing to the rapid one-step synthesis strategy, the structure of CDs in the composites could not be studied separately. Thus, CDs@CA-2 (5 wt%) was introduced for a comparative study with CDs@CA-1. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CDs@CA-1 and CDs@CA-2 show a strong peak at 27.9° caused by the stacking of conjugated aromatic system, which is labelled as the graphitic carbon nitride (002) crystal plane (Fig. 4 a) [54,57]. The rest of the peaks match exactly with the characteristic peaks of CA, implying the formation of CA during the reaction process. As shown in Figure S13 (Supporting Information), UR@CA-0 prepared using only urea has all peaks similar to those of CDs@CA-1 except for a slightly higher relative content of CA. This indicates that CA is produced by the pyrolysis of urea. Notably, the relative intensities of the characteristic peaks of CA in CDs@CA-2 are significantly weaker compared with those of CDs@CA-1, indicating a reduction in the CA content in CDs@CA-2.

The functional groups and chemical composition of CDs@CA-1 and CDs@CA-2 were determined by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The stabilized form of CA in the solid state is s-triazine-2,4,6-trione [58,59]. Thus, the vibrational band observed at 1720 cm−1 in CDs@CA is due to the stretching vibration of the C = O bond of CA (Fig. 4b). Moreover, the stretching vibrational bands observed at 783, 1058, 1400, 3032, and 3211 cm−1 are associated with the triazine ring, C–O, C–N = C, O–H, and N–H, respectively. FT-IR results illustrate that the surface of the material is rich in functional groups. The XPS data show that CDs@CA contains the elements C, N, and O (Fig. 4c). The carbon content of CDs@CA-2 is higher than that of CDs@CA-1, demonstrating the involvement of IPA in the reaction (Table S2, Supporting Information). The C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s XPS spectra of CDs@CA-1 and CDs@CA-2 were deconvoluted and fitted (Fig. 4d–f). The C 1 s spectra can be fitted to four binding energies of 289.47, 288.89, 286.16, and 284.48 eV assigned to C = O, N–C = N, C–N/C–O, and C–C/C = C, respectively (Fig. 4d). The XPS spectrum of N 1 s exhibits two peaks at binding energies of 400.37 (N–(C)3) and 399.47 eV (C–N = C) (Fig. 4e). The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum contains two peaks at 532.8and 531.55 eV corresponding to C–O and C = O bonds, respectively (Fig. 4f). To determine the relative changes in chemical groups, all XPS deconvolution and fitting results for CDs@CA-1 and CDs@CA-2 were collected and displayed in Table S3 (Supporting Information). The results indicate that increasing IPA content leads to a significant reduction in C = O levels in CDs@CA-2, suggesting a decrease in CA content.

From the above discussion, we can draw the following conclusions. As urea undergoes heating, it produces CA acting as an electron acceptor in the binary donor–acceptor exciplex system. CDs, which are produced by the reaction of IPA with urea (or CA produced by urea), act as the electron donor. The combination of the two generates strong CS state. Moreover, the high proportion of IPA results in the production of more CDs and the consumption of more CA. Consequently, this leads to a reduced distance between the CDs radical cation and the CA radical anion within the material, thus weakening the separation degree of the CS states. The final result is a shortening of LPL duration (Fig. 3g).

CA serves as the electron acceptor in this OLPL system, and its relative content in CDs@CA is crucial to the LPL performance. Therefore, under identical experimental conditions, a certain quality of CA was additionally added into the preparation process of CDs@CA-1 and CDs@CA-2 for the reaction to investigate its impact on LPL performance. In Figure S14 (Supporting Information), the changes are observed in afterglow intensity of the samples made from CDs@CA-1 and CDs@CA-2 with the addition of different masses of CA. It can be found regardless of CDs@CA-1 or CDs@CA-2, with the increase of CA addition, the afterglow intensity of the prepared samples shows a trend of increasing and then decreasing. Surprisingly, the afterglow intensity of the sample obtained by adding 0.5 g of CA to the preparation of CDs@CA-2 (named CDs#CA-2) is greatly improved. The afterglow intensity of CDs#CA-2 is increased by 66 % with respect to CDs@CA-2. From the decay spectrum and LPL photographs of CDs#CA-2 (Fig. 4g and Figure S15, Supporting Information), it can be seen that the LPL duration of CDs#CA-2 is also enhanced substantially. This is likely due to the optimal level of separation of the CS state achieved by the material's electron donor to electron acceptor ratio after the addition of CA, resulting in improved LPL performance. On the other hand, CDs@CA-1 may have already achieved its optimal electron donor to electron acceptor ratios prior to the introduction of CA, thus the optical properties are not significantly improved with the addition of CA. The structure of CDs#CA-2 was characterized. From the XRD pattern of CDs#CA-2, it can be seen that with respected to CDs@CA-2, CDs#CA-2 is significantly enhanced in the relative intensity of the characteristic peak of CA (Fig. 4h). In the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s of CDs#CA-2, a significant increase in the proportion of C = O is also observed (Figure S16, Supporting Information). XRD and XPS results confirm the incorporation of CA. The enhanced properties of CDs#CA-2 reveal the prominent function of CA as an electron acceptor in the material, as well as the significant impact of the donor–acceptor ratio on the properties of the CS state in binary donor–acceptor exciplex systems. It should be noticed that, since the electron acceptor and electron donor in the system cannot be completely separated, it is not possible to study them separately and thus explore LPL more. In addition, the LPL of inorganic materials is usually determined by the type and distribution of traps, and the study of traps in the future would be beneficial to broadening our understanding on CDs-based OLPL materials.

In order to further validate our synthesis strategy, other carbon sources with structural similarity to IPA, such as trimesic acid, phthalic acid, terephthalic acid, and 3-aminobenzoic acid, were selected for the synthesis experiments. The above carbon sources mixed with urea were used to synthesize CDs@CA-a, CDs@CA-b, CDs@CA-c, and CDs@CA-d, respectively, under otherwise unchanged experimental conditions. The PL spectra indicate that their optimal PL emission peaks are located at 434, 438, 450, and 420 nm, respectively (Figure S17, Supporting Information). Similarly, as shown in Figure S18 (Supporting Information), their optimal afterglow emission peaks are located at 435, 452, 450, and 446 nm, respectively (The lifetimes under optimal excitation wavelength are 1.21, 0.80, 0.95, and 1.56 s, respectively, Figure S19 and Table S4, Supporting Information). Under optimal excitation, all of them exhibit blue afterglow emission (Figure S20, Supporting Information). On the other hand, they do not present the LPL in adjustable colors. Their LPL decay profiles were tested. All show characteristics of the inverse power function of time t−1, indicating the generation of CS state in the process of UV light irradiation (Fig. 4i) [10]. Similarly, they exhibit LPL of different durations under irradiation with a handheld UV lamp at 275 nm (Figure S21, Supporting Information). These examples illustrate the universal applicability of our one-step synthesis strategy. This method opens up new ideas for the preparation of CDs-based OLPL composites in the future.

CDs@CA exhibits outstanding storage and thermal stability. CDs@CA-1 showed a negligible reduction in afterglow intensity after 7 months of storage under ambient conditions or 24 h at 373 K (Figures S22 and S23, Supporting Information). In general, as the CS state is unstable in water, OLPL materials usually require a rigorous curing procedure [10,60,61]. Surprisingly, CDs@CA-1 was still able to exhibit the LPL phenomenon in an aqueous environment, as well as being able to be stored for a long period of time in an aqueous environment (Fig. 5 a). The afterglow spectra of CDs@CA-1 stored in an aqueous environment for over four months showed only a small decrease in the afterglow intensity (Figure S24, Supporting Information).

The potential applications of CDs@CA-1 were explored in continuous information display and fingerprint visualisation as a result of their excellent luminescent properties. For this purpose, we put CDs@CA-1 powder into the “EXIT” and arrow mold, which can display information for up to 60 min after the 275 nm UV light is turned off (Fig. 5b). Next, we dispersed a small amount of CDs@CA-1 powder evenly in petroleum jelly to dip the thumb and press a fingerprint on a paper. When the UV lamp was switched off, we observed a clear fingerprint pattern, as demonstrated in Fig. 5c. The afterglow of the fingerprints was clearly visible for 11 s. After switching off the UV light we could easily distinguish the texture of the fingerprints with the naked eyes. The above results indicate that our CDs-based OLPL materials can be well used for fingerprint visualisation and continuous display of information.

In summary, we have achieved a one-step synthesis of color-tunable CDs-based OLPL materials. CDs and CA are generated in one step during the reaction to form a binary donor–acceptor exciplex system. This system can produce robust CS state under ultraviolet light irradiation, thereby achieving LPL under environmental conditions and even in aqueous media. By changing the excitation wavelength from 275 nm to 365 nm, the color of LPL can change from blue to green. Further studies have found that the presence of multiple emission centers in CDs@CA is the main reason for their LPL color tunability. Meanwhile, the experiment further shows the great role of CA as an electron acceptor in the OLPL system. The ratio of CA to CDs in the system directly affects the separation degree of CS state, which in turn causes the change of LPL duration. Finally, the versatility of the one-step synthesis strategy for CDs@CA was verified by selecting structurally similar carbon sources, which would effectively expand the scope of related research and application of CDs-based OLPL materials.

Urea was purchased from Greagent. Iso-phthalic acid (IPA), cyanuric acid (CA), trimesic acid (TMA), phthalic acid (PA), terephthalic acid (TPA), and 3-aminobenzoic acid (3ABA) were purchased from Adamas. All the chemicals used in this work were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

A certain amount of IPA (6, 100, 250, or 500 mg; IPA:urea by weight is 0.3 %, 5 %, 12.5 %, and 25 %) was added to urea (2 g), and the mixture of urea with IPA was well ground at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous mixture of white powders. The white powder mixture was placed in a 30 mL round crucible and heated in oven at 195 °C air atmosphere for 5 h. After the reaction was finished and cooled to room temperature, the solid sample in the crucible was collected and ground into powder form to obtain OLPL composites with different loading amounts of CDs (named CDs@CA-1, CDs@CA-2, CDs@CA-3, and CDs@CA-4, respectively).
UR@CA-0 was synthesized without IPA, and other conditions were identical with those of CDs@CA.

IPA (100 mg) and CA (500 mg) were added to urea (2 g), and the mixture of urea with IPA and CA was well ground at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous mixture of white powders. The subsequent steps were the same as for CDs@CA-2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs@CA-a, CDs@CA-b, CDs@CA-c, and CDs@CA-d
ce:section.ce:para: By replacing IPA with TMA, PA, TPA, and 3ABA, CDs@CA-a, CDs@CA-b, CDs@CA-c, and CDs@CA-d were obtained under identical reaction conditions for CDs@CA-1.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on an F200 (JEOL). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured on a K-Alpha (Thermo Scientific). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on an X’Pert PRO MPD. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a TENSOR 27 (Bruker). UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on a UV-8000 (Metash). Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were measured on a A300 (Bruker). PL emission and excitation spectra, room temperature afterglow emission and excitation spectra, and afterglow lifetime decay spectra were recorded on a fluorescence F7100 (Hitachi). Temperature-dependent afterglow emission spectra were recorded on a fluorescence FLS 1000 (Edinburgh). Fluorescence quantum yield was measured on an FLS 1000 (Edinburgh) with an integrating sphere under an optimal excitation wavelength. Photographs of PL and afterglow were taken on a D700 camera (Nikon) under excitation by a hand-hold UV lamp. Depending on the monitor, some afterglow photos may not be displayed in their entirety. It could be easier to view these photos with other monitors or print them out for viewing. The phosphorescence quantum yield was calculated by the previously reported method [52].

Yujie Di: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Weifeng Liu: Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Shuhao Shi: Investigation, Methodology. Tao Wu: Methodology. Meiling Wang: Investigation. Xuguang Liu: Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51972221), Central Leading Science and Technology Development Foundation of Shanxi Province (YDZJSX2022A009), Key R&D Program of Shanxi Province (International Cooperation, 201903D421077).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.147589.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long persistent phosphors—from fundamentals to applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Li, M. Gecevicius, J. Qiu, Long persistent phosphors—from fundamentals to applications, Chem. Soc. Rev. 45(8) (2016) 2090-2136. doi: 10.1039/c5cs00582e.

Wang, Graphitic Carbon Nitride Quantum Dots Embedded in Carbon Nanosheets for Near-Infrared Imaging-Guided Combined Photo Chemotherapy, ACS Nano 14(10) (2020) 13304-13315. doi: 10.1021/acsnano.0c05143.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Theoretical study on the mechanism of keto–enol isomerization for cyanuric acid and cyameluric acid
